flashcards.q: When did Mahatma Gandhi return to India from South Africa?
flashcards.a: January 1915.

flashcards.q: What is the core idea of Satyagraha?
flashcards.a: It emphasizes the power of truth and the need to search for truth, suggesting that physical force is not necessary to fight injustice.

flashcards.q: Name the three early Satyagraha movements organized by Gandhiji in India.
flashcards.a: Champaran (1917), Kheda (1917), and Ahmedabad (1918).

flashcards.q: What was the purpose of the Rowlatt Act (1919)?
flashcards.a: It gave the government enormous powers to repress political activities and allowed detention of political prisoners without trial for two years.

flashcards.q: On which date did the Jallianwala Bagh massacre take place?
flashcards.a: 13 April 1919.

flashcards.q: Who was the British officer responsible for the Jallianwala Bagh firing?
flashcards.a: General Dyer.

flashcards.q: Why was the Khilafat Committee formed in 1919?
flashcards.a: To defend the temporal powers of the Khalifa, the spiritual head of the Islamic world, after the defeat of Ottoman Turkey in WWI.

flashcards.q: In which book did Gandhiji declare that British rule in India survived only due to the cooperation of Indians?
flashcards.a: Hind Swaraj (1909).

flashcards.q: At which Congress session was the Non-Cooperation programme finally adopted?
flashcards.a: Nagpur session, December 1920.

flashcards.q: What event led Gandhiji to withdraw the Non-Cooperation Movement in February 1922?
flashcards.a: The violent incident at Chauri Chaura.

flashcards.q: Who formed the Swaraj Party within the Congress?
flashcards.a: C.R. Das and Motilal Nehru.

flashcards.q: Why was the Simon Commission (1928) boycotted by Indians?
flashcards.a: Because it did not have a single Indian member; all its members were British.

flashcards.q: In which session did the Congress formalize the demand for 'Purna Swaraj'?
flashcards.a: Lahore session (December 1929), under the presidency of Jawaharlal Nehru.

flashcards.q: What was the significance of 26 January 1930 in the nationalist movement?
flashcards.a: It was declared as Independence Day, when people were to take a pledge to struggle for complete independence.

flashcards.q: How many miles was the Salt March from Sabarmati to Dandi?
flashcards.a: Over 240 miles.

flashcards.q: When and how did Gandhiji ceremonially violate the salt law?
flashcards.a: On 6 April 1930, by manufacturing salt by boiling sea water at Dandi.

flashcards.q: Who was known as the 'Frontier Gandhi'?
flashcards.a: Abdul Ghaffar Khan.

flashcards.q: What was the Gandhi-Irwin Pact (March 1931)?
flashcards.a: An agreement where Gandhiji consented to participate in a Round Table Conference and the government agreed to release political prisoners.

flashcards.q: Who were the leaders of the tribal peasant movement in the Gudem Hills of Andhra Pradesh?
flashcards.a: Alluri Sitaram Raju.

flashcards.q: What was the Inland Emigration Act of 1859?
flashcards.a: A law that prohibited plantation workers from leaving tea gardens without permission, which was rarely granted.

flashcards.q: Which organization did B.R. Ambedkar form for Dalits in 1930?
flashcards.a: Depressed Classes Association.

flashcards.q: What was the outcome of the Poona Pact (September 1932)?
flashcards.a: It gave reserved seats to Depressed Classes in provincial and central legislative councils, to be voted by the general electorate.

flashcards.q: Who first visually associated the identity of India with the image of Bharat Mata?
flashcards.a: Bankim Chandra Chattopadhyay (later painted by Abanindranath Tagore).

flashcards.q: What were the colors of the Swaraj flag designed by Gandhiji in 1921?
flashcards.a: Red, green, and white.

flashcards.q: When was the 'Quit India' resolution passed by the Congress Working Committee?
flashcards.a: 14 July 1942 (at Wardha); later endorsed on 8 August 1942 (in Bombay).
